refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 as stated is a KPI support requirement but lacks specificity on measurement scope, boundary conditions, and system responsibilities. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a measurable outcome dependent on operator behavior, vehicle efficiency, teleoperation overhead, and safety system overhead. The FR now explicitly bounds the evaluation period and operating envelope, making it verifiable and traceable to design decisions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish baseline energy consumption (kWh) and material moved (tons) across representative worksite scenarios. Measure energy_per_ton at regular intervals during the evaluation period. Compare against E_target. Decompose variance into vehicle efficiency, teleoperation network overhead, safety system overhead, and operator behavior to identify design vs. operational improvement opportunities.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The Vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit of work output (ton-move) through efficient drivetrain, hydraulics, and load management.
FR-EE-002: The Teleoperation system shall minimize network and processing overhead energy cost relative to the teleoperation session duration and data throughput.
FR-EE-003: The Safety Services system shall execute safety monitoring and intervention logic with minimal energy overhead.
FR-EE-004: The NGHE Solution shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and efficiency metrics to operators and site managers to enable behavioral optimization.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE Solution shall support energy efficiency measurement and reporting aligned with the customer-defined evaluation period and E_target acceptance criteria.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement energy-efficient vehicle subsystems (motor, hydraulics, transmission) with efficiency targets allocated per subsystem; monitor aggregate energy consumption via onboard telemetry; report energy_per_ton KPI to site manager dashboard.

DP-B: Design vehicle, teleoperation, and safety systems with explicit energy budgets; implement real-time energy accounting across all subsystems; provide operator feedback loops and automated efficiency optimization (e.g., load balancing, idle reduction); measure and report energy_per_ton against E_target with variance attribution.

DP-C: Optimize vehicle mechanical efficiency only; assume teleoperation and safety overhead are negligible; focus design on drivetrain and load management; measure energy_per_ton as vehicle energy divided by material moved, ignoring system overhead.

DP-D: Implement a hierarchical energy management architecture: vehicle efficiency layer (drivetrain, hydraulics), teleoperation efficiency layer (network optimization, compression), safety efficiency layer (predictive vs. reactive monitoring), and a cross-system energy orchestrator that balances performance, safety, and efficiency trade-offs in real time.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hierarchical energy management architecture with explicit energy budgets and trade-off orchestration across vehicle, teleoperation, and safety subsystems, coupled with real-time energy accounting, operator feedback, and automated efficiency optimization to achieve and sustain energy_per_ton ≤ E_target.

potential_sources: Energy trade-off between safety monitoring intensity and efficiency: higher safety overhead may reduce energy_per_ton if it prevents accidents that would consume more energy in recovery/repair.
Teleoperation latency vs. energy: lower-latency teleoperation may enable more efficient operator control, but higher network bandwidth increases energy cost.
Vehicle load optimization vs. safety: aggressive load balancing improves efficiency but must not compromise stability or safety margins.
Operator behavior feedback vs. autonomy: real-time efficiency metrics may encourage aggressive operation that increases safety risk.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D acknowledges these couplings explicitly through the cross-system energy orchestrator. The design does not assume independence; rather, it provides a decision framework to manage trade-offs. Coupling is acceptable if the orchestrator logic is well-defined and validated.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: E_target is achievable with current vehicle technology and teleoperation network infrastructure; if not, design will fail acceptance regardless of optimization.
ASSUMPTION: Operator behavior is a significant variable in energy_per_ton; training and feedback mechanisms are in scope for the NGHE solution.
RISK: Energy orchestrator logic may become complex and difficult to validate; incorrect trade-off decisions could violate safety constraints while pursuing efficiency.
RISK: Real-time energy accounting and feedback loops introduce computational overhead that may itself consume significant energy, reducing net efficiency gain.
RISK: Teleoperation network overhead is highly dependent on site connectivity and latency; if network is poor, teleoperation energy cost may exceed E_target margin, making the KPI unachievable.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define: (1) energy budget allocation across vehicle, teleoperation, and safety subsystems as a function of E_target; (2) orchestrator decision logic and constraints (e.g., safety constraints are non-negotiable); (3) measurement boundary (what energy is included in 'energy_used'—vehicle only, or vehicle + teleoperation + safety?); (4) validation strategy for orchestrator logic to ensure safety and efficiency goals are not in conflict.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and the energy management orchestrator. It owns the energy efficiency KPI measurement, reporting, and cross-system trade-off logic. It is responsible for ensuring energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over the evaluation period.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption data (kWh), material moved (tons), energy_per_ton KPI, subsystem efficiency metrics, and variance attribution are transmitted via Telemetry to site manager dashboard and customer reporting systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Site manager and operator tasking inputs (e.g., load targets, efficiency optimization mode, evaluation period definition) are received via Tasking interface to configure energy management orchestrator behavior.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Energy orchestrator receives SafetyAlert signals from Safety Services indicating safety constraints or risk levels; orchestrator may adjust efficiency optimization to respect safety margins and may signal efficiency-related safety concerns (e.g., low battery) back to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link to coordinate teleoperation and safety services; energy accounting includes Network Link overhead as part of system energy consumption.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the energy efficiency KPI and orchestrator logic. Energy accounting and trade-off decisions span all subsystems, so containment at this level is appropriate. Minimal diffusion: only the measurement and reporting logic is centralized here; subsystem efficiency improvements are owned by Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services respectively.

Vehicle owns drivetrain, hydraulics, and load management efficiency. However, energy_per_ton is a system-level KPI that depends on teleoperation and safety overhead; Vehicle alone cannot achieve the KPI. Containment score reflects that Vehicle is a major contributor but not the sole owner. Diffusion risk: if energy orchestrator logic is distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, consistency and trade-off decisions become difficult.

Teleoperation owns network efficiency and processing overhead. However, teleoperation energy cost is a component of the system-level KPI, not the entire KPI. Containment score reflects partial ownership. Diffusion risk: if energy accounting and orchestrator logic are split between Teleoperation and NGHE Solution, measurement consistency and trade-off decisions may be unclear.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and intervention logic, which has energy overhead. However, safety is a constraint on efficiency optimization, not a primary driver of the energy efficiency KPI. Containment score reflects that Safety Services contributes to energy overhead but does not own the KPI. Diffusion risk: if safety constraints are not clearly communicated to the energy orchestrator, trade-off decisions may violate safety requirements.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module should own the energy efficiency KPI measurement, real-time energy accounting, and cross-system energy orchestrator logic. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own their respective subsystem efficiency improvements and provide energy consumption data to NGHE Solution via Telemetry interface. This containment minimizes diffusion of orchestrator logic and ensures a single source of truth for energy_per_ton calculation and reporting.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution does not have visibility into detailed energy consumption of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems, energy accounting may be inaccurate or incomplete, leading to incorrect KPI measurement.

RISK: If energy orchestrator logic is not clearly separated from subsystem efficiency logic, changes to one subsystem may inadvertently affect orchestrator behavior, introducing coupling and regression risk.

RISK: If NGHE Solution is responsible for energy orchestration but does not have authority to adjust subsystem parameters (e.g., teleoperation latency, safety monitoring intensity), orchestrator recommendations may be ignored, and KPI may not be achieved.
RISK: Energy accounting overhead (computation, storage, transmission) may itself consume significant energy; if not carefully managed, the measurement system may reduce net efficiency.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish clear data contracts between NGHE Solution and subsystems: (1) Vehicle provides real-time energy consumption (kWh) and work output (ton-move) at regular intervals; (2) Teleoperation provides network energy overhead and session duration; (3) Safety Services provides safety monitoring energy overhead and safety constraint status. Define orchestrator authority: what parameters can the orchestrator adjust (e.g., teleoperation compression level, safety monitoring frequency) and what are hard constraints (e.g., safety margins). Implement energy accounting as a lightweight, non-intrusive monitoring layer to minimize overhead.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hierarchical energy management with orchestration) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve energy_per_ton ≤ E_target through system-level optimization). The DP acknowledges the multi-subsystem nature of the problem and provides a logical framework for trade-off management. The DP is not over-constrained and allows for multiple implementation approaches. Coupling risks are identified and manageable through orchestrator design.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: If energy orchestrator logic is distributed across NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, consistency and maintainability become difficult. Mitigation: centralize orchestrator logic in NGHE Solution; subsystems provide data and accept orchestrator directives.

DIFFUSION RISK: If energy accounting is implemented separately in each subsystem, measurement inconsistencies may arise. Mitigation: implement energy accounting as a single, centralized service in NGHE Solution that aggregates subsystem data.

DIFFUSION RISK: If safety constraints are not clearly communicated to the energy orchestrator, trade-off decisions may inadvertently violate safety requirements. Mitigation: establish formal safety constraint interface (SafetyAlert) between Safety Services and NGHE Solution.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for the energy efficiency KPI and orchestrator logic. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are contained within NGHE Solution and provide data and accept directives via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Containment is clear and diffusion is minimized.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Energy_per_ton can be measured directly from Vehicle energy consumption (via Telemetry) and material moved (via Tasking or Vehicle sensors). Evaluation period is defined by customer and communicated via Tasking. E_target is a customer-defined acceptance criterion. Verification is feasible if subsystems provide accurate energy and work output data.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The refined FR is allocated to NGHE Solution as the top-level owner of the KPI. Sub-FRs are allocated to Vehicle (drivetrain efficiency), Teleoperation (network efficiency), Safety Services (safety overhead), and NGHE Solution (measurement, reporting, orchestration). Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Clarify the measurement boundary for 'energy_used': does it include only Vehicle energy, or also Teleoperation and Safety Services overhead? This affects E_target achievability and must be agreed with the customer.
Define the evaluation period and E_target as formal acceptance criteria in the requirements specification, with clear measurement methodology and acceptance thresholds.
Establish formal safety constraint interface between Safety Services and NGHE Solution to ensure orchestrator respects safety margins.
Define energy orchestrator decision logic and constraints explicitly, including trade-off priorities (e.g., safety > efficiency) and fallback behavior if E_target is unachievable.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-defined and the selected DP is logically sound, but the DP requires decomposition into sub-DPs for Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and the energy orchestrator. The IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate, but the sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) must be decomposed further to define specific design parameters for each subsystem. Additionally, the energy orchestrator logic is complex and requires detailed specification of decision rules, constraints, and validation strategy. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to make the design actionable and verifiable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The Vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit of work output (ton-move) through efficient drivetrain, hydraulics, and load management.
FR-EE-002: The Teleoperation system shall minimize network and processing overhead energy cost relative to the teleoperation session duration and data throughput.
FR-EE-003: The Safety Services system shall execute safety monitoring and intervention logic with minimal energy overhead.
FR-EE-004: The NGHE Solution shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and efficiency metrics to operators and site managers to enable behavioral optimization.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE Solution shall support energy efficiency measurement and reporting aligned with the customer-defined evaluation period and E_target acceptance criteria.

assumptions: E_target is achievable with current vehicle technology and teleoperation network infrastructure.
Operator behavior is a significant variable in energy_per_ton; training and feedback mechanisms are in scope.
Energy orchestrator logic can be designed to respect safety constraints while pursuing efficiency optimization.
Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) will provide accurate energy consumption data to NGHE Solution.
Measurement boundary for 'energy_used' includes Vehicle energy and system overhead (Teleoperation, Safety Services); this will be confirmed with customer.
open_risks: Energy orchestrator logic may become complex and difficult to validate; incorrect trade-off decisions could violate safety constraints.
Real-time energy accounting and feedback loops introduce computational overhead that may reduce net efficiency gain.
Teleoperation network overhead is highly dependent on site connectivity; poor network may make E_target unachievable.
Operator behavior may not respond to efficiency feedback; behavioral change may require additional training or incentives outside the system design.
Safety constraints and efficiency optimization may be in fundamental conflict; trade-off resolution may require customer acceptance of lower efficiency or higher safety risk.
evidence_needed: Customer confirmation of E_target value and measurement boundary (vehicle only vs. vehicle + system overhead).
Baseline energy consumption data from existing equipment or similar systems to validate E_target achievability.
Teleoperation network performance data (latency, bandwidth, reliability) to estimate network energy overhead.
Safety constraint specifications and risk tolerance to define orchestrator constraints.
Operator behavior data and feedback mechanisms to validate efficiency optimization effectiveness.
Validation plan for energy orchestrator logic, including trade-off scenarios and safety constraint verification.
